nodes.title: BOARDROOM DECISIONS
nodes.body: Track all relevant Boardroom decisions and Summaries in one place. These are outputs from the /command, ./ boardroom

April 25, 2026
# Founder Briefing

You asked the board to choose between Stripe Atlas, Gust Launch, Clerky, and any alternative. The board unanimously voted Atlas, but the more important takeaway is what that vote actually says: this is the right decision, but it's also the smallest decision in front of you right now.

## The decision
Use Stripe Atlas to incorporate RAV as a Delaware C-Corp.

## The verdict
Unanimous YES. Brian was the only conditional vote in Round 1 and upgraded to YES in Round 2 after the parallel-workstreams framing was clarified.

## The core tension
The board agreed on Atlas, but they disagreed sharply on how much you should be thinking about it. Yokum, Patrick, and Tonia framed Atlas as having specific, non-fungible advantages (clean cap table for due diligence, pre-cleared Stripe live activation, litigation-defense bench effect). Brian and Jason argued it's industry-invisible and the founder is over-weighting it because it has a clear forking decision and a clear price tag, while the harder workstreams (Florida timeshare regulatory opinion, marketplace-lawyer policy review, resort-brand outreach, ARDA membership) get less mental attention because they're harder to scope.

### The sharpest insight in this debate
Patrick's framing — "Atlas is a payments-infrastructure decision wearing a corporate-formation costume." Pre-cleared Stripe live activation through Atlas eliminates 2-10 days of manual underwriting friction that an independently-applied Stripe account for a brand-new timeshare-rental marketplace entity is going to face. That alone is worth the entire $500 fee. Tonia's secondary insight — that you're spending decision-energy on the smaller question because the bigger one (which marketplace lawyer, what to ask them, on what timeline) is harder to scope — is the diagnostic that should reorient your week.

## Recommended action — this week, in parallel:
1. Sign a one-page founder term sheet today documenting the 4-way equity split (Sujit, Ajumon, Sandhya, Celin) and the planned 5th founder reserve. All four signatures.
2. Start Atlas filing — the same week. Sujit and Sandhya issue founder shares immediately; Ajumon and Celin's allocations stay reserved until OBA disclosures clear.
3. Engage a Florida marketplace lawyer (specifically with timeshare and platform-law experience — Greenberg Traurig Orlando hospitality practice or Holland & Knight vacation ownership group are Brian's recommendations). Hand them the 8 policy drafts in docs/support/policies/ AND ask for an opinion on PaySafe's money-transmission licensing status AND ask for the Florida Vacation Plan and Timesharing Act regulatory opinion.
4. Begin proactive brand-protection outreach to Hilton Grand Vacations, Marriott Vacations Worldwide, and Disney Vacation Club within 30 days of incorporation.
5. Defer ARDA membership to month 6 (per Brian's negotiation with Jason).

## The dissent worth heeding
Brian's Round 1 concern, which he upgraded out of in Round 2 but which still matters: do not let "Atlas filing complete" become equivalent to "platform is launch-ready." The corporate shell is necessary, not sufficient. Onboarding owners before the lawyer review of the 8 policy drafts is complete and before the timeshare regulatory opinion is in hand is the failure mode the board is collectively most worried about — even though everyone voted YES on the formation choice.

# What's ready for you to use

---

## Documents and Templates

### 1. Founder Term Sheet
`docs/incorporation/templates/founder-term-sheet.md`

The pre-incorporation document all four founders sign before clicking Atlas. It covers the full Yokum checklist:
- 4-way equity split with [XX%] placeholders + decision-guide TODOs (the equity-split number is the single most important decision; I left it as a TODO since the board explicitly didn't opine on it)
- Vesting locked to 4-year/1-year cliff (Atlas default; don't change)
- 83(b) election commitment with the 30-day deadline call-out
- OBA-blocker handling for Ajumon and Celin — stock issuance deferred but IP assignment immediate (Yokum's specific advice from Round 1)
- Future 5th-founder framework — true founder if joins ≤90 days, option grant if 90 days–6 months, reservation rolls into pool if not
- Board composition — sole CEO director default with note that OBA-blocked founders should NOT take initial board seats (could trigger conflict at their employers)

### 2. Intro Email to Lawyer Outreach
`docs/incorporation/lawyer-outreach/intro-email.md`

Two versions ready to send: cold (Version A) and warm intro (Version B). Both under 250 words. Includes:
- Sending checklist (founder term sheet signed, Atlas filing started, etc.)
- Target firm shortlist with rationale (`Greenberg Traurig`, `Holland & Knight`, `Foley & Lardner`, `Baker & Hostetler`, plus the explicit "Cooley = free incorporation review only, NOT timeshare/platform work" note)
- Strategy: send cold to 2 firms, work warm intros for the others, aim for engagement letter within 4 weeks.

### 3. RFP Document for Incorporation Legal Work
`docs/incorporation/lawyer-outreach/rfp.md`

Full scope-of-work document. Three workstreams scoped:
1. Florida Vacation Plan / Timesharing Act regulatory opinion (`Section 721.20`) — estimated at 5-10 hours.
2. Platform document review (`8 policy drafts`) + drafting (`Owner Listing Agreement`, `AUP`, `Marketplace Operator Agreement`) — estimated at 25-40 hours.
3. PaySafe MSB licensing opinion in **5 high-inventory states** — estimated at 5-10 hours.

Budget envelope explicitly stated as `$10K-$25K` total. Fixed-fee preferred. Includes a "Questions for you" section asking the firm to disclose conflicts (e.g., current/recent rep of major timeshare developers).

### 4. Attorney Referral Letter for Personal Outreach
`docs/incorporation/personal-outreach/attorney-referral-letter.md`

Reusable letter for friends/contacts with four tone variants baked in:
1. Main letter — close friend/family (warm, multi-paragraph)
2. Acquaintance — tighter opening for less-close contacts.
3. Hospitality industry contact — skips market explainer (they get it).
4. Lawyer/law-adjacent contact — concrete, ask-focused.

Each variant is forwardable so the recipient can broker the intro without a follow-up call. Includes a tracking convention (`letters-sent/subfolder + referrals-received.md log`).

---

## Decisions Pending Before Sending Anything:
1. **Equity Split Percentages** — The term sheet has [XX%] placeholders. Locking this is the single hardest pre-incorporation conversation among the four founders.
   - The board didn't weigh in; it's a founders' decision.
   - Common patterns include: equal (25/25/25/25), weighted-toward-technical (35/25/20/20), weighted-toward-CEO (40/25/20/15).
   - Worth a separate conversation among all four of you.
2. **Founder Backgrounds** — The RFP has `[Background to fill in]` for all four founders.
   - Lawyers read this to gauge fit.
   - Even 1-2 sentences each are enough.
3. **Friend's Name + Phone/Email** — Placeholders `[FRIEND'S NAME]`, `[PHONE]`, `[EMAIL]` need filling.

  ---
  Process flow recap (your earlier question, now operationalized)

  Per the boardroom: not 3 sequential steps but 5 parallel workstreams starting same week:

  Week 1: 
           ├─ Sign founder term sheet (3-day decision window for the 4 of you)
           ├─ Start Atlas filing (same day as term sheet signing)
           ├─ Send personal-network letters to 5-10 contacts
           ├─ Send cold outreach to 2 firms (Greenberg Traurig + Holland & Knight)
           └─ (no marketplace lawyer engaged yet — outreach in flight)

  Week 2: 
           ├─ Atlas Cert arrives + EIN + Stripe live + Mercury bank
           ├─ Lawyer responses come back; pick one based on fit + budget
           ├─ Sign engagement letter
           └─ File Florida foreign-entity registration (form CR2E047, ~$200)

  Week 3:  
           ├─ Lawyer kicks off Workstream 1 (regulatory opinion) + Workstream 2 (doc review)
           ├─ Sandhya + Sujit begin proactive brand-protection outreach to HGV/MVW/DVC
           └─ Atlas + Mercury fully operational; Stripe live taking test transactions

  Week 4-6:
            ├─ Lawyer delivers regulatory opinion + redlined policy docs
            ├─ Brand outreach responses come in
            └─ Internal review of lawyer's redlines

  Week 7-8:
            ├─ Final sign-off on full packet
            ├─ Switch policy docs from status: draft → status: active
            └─ Lift Staff Only Mode → onboard first owner

nodes.title: STRIPE, PAYMENTS AND understanding PAYSAFE
nodes.body:
# Analysis of Money Transmission and Risk

On his point about money transmission — I think he was correct in his analysis but understated the risk. He stated:

> "The standard structure for marketplace platforms is to use an established money services business... PaySafe is the right answer here if PaySafe is what I think it is."

I want to flag that the founder needs a written legal opinion on whether PaySafe's escrow service is itself a licensed money services business in every state RAV operates in. If PaySafe is unlicensed in a particular state, RAV's use of PaySafe to hold funds for transactions in that state may inherit the unlicensed transmission exposure.

This is not a theoretical risk. **The Conference of State Bank Supervisors** has been increasingly aggressive on marketplace escrow arrangements over the past three years.

# Analysis of Escrow Provider and Alternatives

The fact that **PaySafe** is the named escrow provider and we cannot collectively verify whether PaySafe is a licensed money services business in every state RAV operates in — that's a **red flag**, not a yellow flag.

When I said "PaySafe is the right answer if PaySafe is what I think it is," I was being too generous with the founder's prior research.

## Specific Legal and Compliance Questions
The marketplace attorney engagement needs to specifically include:
1. Is PaySafe licensed as an MSB in Florida, California, Hawaii, Nevada, and South Carolina at minimum?
2. If not, does RAV's use of PaySafe for transactions in those states inherit unlicensed transmission exposure?
3. What is the alternative if PaySafe is not licensed where it needs to be?

## Alternative Solution: Stripe Connect
The alternative — and this is where my Stripe lens matters — is to use **Stripe Connect**'s own marketplace escrow capabilities.
- With Stripe Connect destination charges, you can hold funds in the platform account until you're ready to release them via Transfer to the connected account.
- This functions as escrow without using a third-party escrow provider.
- Stripe has the licensing in place because Stripe itself is a licensed money transmitter in the relevant states.
- RAV doesn't inherit transmission exposure from using Stripe's standard products in their standard ways.

## Implementation Considerations
If PaySafe turns out to be the wrong choice or unlicensed where needed:
- Replacing it with a **Stripe Connect-native escrow flow** is a 2-3 week engineering project.
- The `handler.ts` files reviewed in your test PR are well-structured enough that this swap is feasible.
- First, get the legal opinion.
- If PaySafe survives the legal review, keep it.
- If not, plan for migration.

nodes.title: DISCUSSION: Approaching large brands
nodes.body:
DISCUSS the approach between 

Should RAV **operate quietly for 6-12 months under the radar** to establish  transaction volume before asking for permission, or signal participation to the industry from day one?"

# The Case for Going Quiet First

You build proof-of-traction (booking volume, owner satisfaction, traveler reviews) before you ask any major brand to acknowledge your existence. When you eventually approach Hilton or Marriott, you're not pitching "we're a brand-new platform, please don't object" — you're pitching "we've facilitated 5,000 transactions on properties associated with your brand over the past 12 months, with X% NPS, Y% chargeback rate, Z% repeat usage." That's a much stronger position.

# The Case for Being Public from Day One

The major brands have brand-protection teams that will eventually find your platform whether you announce yourself or not. If they find you and your first interaction is them sending a cease-and-desist, your starting position is adversarial.

If you proactively reach out and have a 30-minute conversation with their brand-protection contact early, your starting position is "they know us, they expect to monitor us, they have not objected." The cease-and-desist becomes much less likely. And when it eventually comes (some brand will eventually push back), you have an existing relationship to negotiate within.

# Industry-Specific Considerations for RAV

For RAV specifically — and this is where my industry experience matters — Hilton Grand Vacations and Marriott Vacations Worldwide both have well-established brand-protection processes. They monitor secondary rental platforms.

KOALA partnering with Expedia Group in May 2024 put this entire category on the brand-protection radar in a way it wasn't before. It is essentially impossible for RAV to operate at any scale without these brands becoming aware of it. The question is timing — do they become aware on RAV's terms (proactive outreach, framed conversation) or on theirs (a cease-and-desist letter triggered by a brand-monitoring alert)?

# Recommended Approach: Proactive Outreach

I'm voting for proactive. Specifically: send a low-key introductory email to the brand-protection contact at Hilton Grand Vacations within 30 days of incorporation. Not a partnership pitch. Not a press release. A short email saying:
- "We've launched a platform that may feature properties associated with your brand;"
- "We want to ensure compliance with your brand usage guidelines;"
- "Here's how to reach us if you have questions."
Keep it on file. Repeat with Marriott Vacations Worldwide and Disney Vacation Club.

# ARDA Membership Strategy

On ARDA membership — Brian's right that I have institutional reasons to want startup members. I'll concede that the $5-15K is real and the founder may legitimately want to defer until there's transaction volume to point to.

The compromise position: apply for ARDA membership in month 6 once you have 1,000+ transactions. The membership will be approved faster (you're a real company by then) and the credibility lift from "ARDA member with documented transaction volume" is higher than "ARDA member who just incorporated."

nodes.title: DISCUSSION POINTS related to Incorporation
nodes.body:
# On the 4-founder structure

**Industry-side, this is mildly positive.**

- A solo founder with a marketplace platform looks like a hobbyist.
- A 4-person team with assigned C-suite roles looks like a company.

Make sure the LinkedIn presence and the website's "About" page actually reflect the four founders publicly — when industry contacts (resort brand business development teams, ARDA members, hospitality conference speakers) check who's behind RAV, they will look at LinkedIn first.

If only Sujit shows up publicly, the platform looks like a one-person side project regardless of what the corporate filing says.

# OBA Blocker for Ajumon and Celin

I want to flag this as an **industry-credibility concern**, not just a **corporate-mechanics concern**. If two of your four founders' employment situations would be viewed by a regulated financial institution as creating an **Outside Business Activity (OBA)** conflict, that's not a fatal pattern, but it does mean those two founders cannot publicly represent RAV at industry events — such as:

- ARDA conferences
- Hospitality industry conferences
- Investor pitches

until their OBAs are cleared. Plan for this. The two cleared founders should be the public face for the next 6-12 months.

# Industry Positioning Post-Incorporation

Once Atlas filing completes and RAV is a Delaware C-Corp registered to do business in Florida, the next industry-credibility step is **ARDA membership**.

- ARDA membership costs between **$5K-$15K**, depending on company size.
- It is not required to operate a timeshare rental platform.

**What ARDA membership signals to the industry:**
- That RAV understands it operates within the timeshare ecosystem.
- That RAV intends to be a participant rather than an outsider.
- It opens doors for networking and partnerships.

**Industry Events:**
- Marriott Vacations Worldwide and Hilton Grand Vacations both attend ARDA's annual conference.
- The next conference is scheduled for **October 2026** at the Westin Kierland Resort in Phoenix.

**Strategic Opportunity:**
- If RAV becomes an ARDA member by then, the founder team can request in-person meetings with their corporate development teams.
- If not, those meetings will occur via cold outreach with much lower success rates.

# Immediate Operational Action Plan

## If I Were the Founder, Here's Exactly What I Would Do This Week:

1. Sign the founder term sheet (Yokum's point)
2. Start Atlas filing
3. Engage a Florida marketplace attorney for:
   - Policy review (Tonia's point)
   - Timeshare regulatory opinion (Brian's point)

*Note:* These can be the same firm if you find one with both benches.

4. Email Hilton Grand Vacations brand protection (Jason's point)
5. Begin drafting the chargeback dispute response template that you'll use the first time a traveler chargebacks a booking — because they will, and the response template is what defends the chargeback.

Discuss details on How to reach out to Hilton Grand Vacations brand protection
ACTION ITEMS: Research Contacts- names, email IDs etc
ACTION ITEMS: Draft email for contacting, discuss what to share with respect to incorporation status etc and send email and follow up

nodes.title: IMPORTANT: Regulatory Status and Compliance Considerations for RAV
nodes.body:
**LITIGATION-TEST of R-A-V POLICY AND TOS documents**

 **- Booking ToS:** 
- Does it disclaim that RAV is a party to the rental transaction? 
- Does it require binding individual arbitration with class waiver?
- Does it specify jurisdiction (Delaware vs Florida vs the user's home state — this matters)? Does it have a survivability clause for if the user account is later deleted?

  **- Privacy Policy:** 

-   Does it accurately describe every data flow? Stripe, Resend, Sentry, PostHog, GA4, OpenRouter, VAPI, Deepgram, ElevenLabs —   
  every vendor that touches user data must be disclosed by category. 
- Does it disclose that voice and chat conversations are stored? 
- Does it explain how to request deletion? Does it explain the 30-day deletion timeline?
 
**Cancellation Policy:** 

- Does it match what the platform actually does (flexible/moderate/strict/super_strict)? The single most common source of consumer class actions in this category is "the cancellation policy as written doesn't match the cancellation policy as enforced."

**Refund Policy:** 

- Where does the money go in each scenario? Who decides? On what timeline? Patrick will hammer this point and he's right to.     

**Trust & Safety Policy:** 

- Does it set the framework for owner removal, traveler banning, and dispute resolution? Procedurally fair? Documented appeals process?

**Insurance/Liability:** 

- Does it allocate risk between RAV, owner, and traveler in each plausible failure mode? 
  - Resort refuses check-in. 
  - Property is materially different from listing. 
  - Traveler damages property. 
  - Owner cancels last-minute.

**Payment Policy:** 
Does it explain PaySafe escrow mechanics in plain English? 
Does it disclose Stripe's terms apply to payment processing? 
Does it explain what happens if Stripe initiates a chargeback?

**Subscription Terms:** 

- Does it address auto-renewal in compliance with the FTC's Negative Option Rule (which the FTC formally adopted in October 2024)? California's automatic renewal law? 
- 30-day notice for price changes?

# Regulatory Status and Compliance Considerations for RAV

## Florida Vacation Plan and Timesharing Act
The platform's regulatory status under the Florida Vacation Plan and Timesharing Act requires a **written legal opinion** before launch. If RAV is determined to be operating as an unlicensed timeshare resale service provider, the penalty under **Section 721.20(11)** is **$5,000 per violation** — and each transaction can constitute a separate violation.

Even if the legal opinion ultimately concludes that RAV is not a resale service provider (which is the likely conclusion given that you're a rental platform, not a resale broker), the opinion letter itself remains an asset in any future regulatory inquiry or industry due-diligence conversation.

## FTC Guidance on Timeshare Advertising (2023)
I would also flag that the **FTC issued updated guidance in 2023** regarding timeshare advertising practices. Specific compliance requirements apply to:
- Pricing claims
- Availability claims
- Testimonials

Your **RAV Smart Suite tools** (SmartEarn, SmartPrice, SmartCompare) and any pricing guidance displayed to owners must adhere to these guidelines. This requirement stands regardless of whether you incorporate via Atlas or any other platform.

# On the Resort-Brand Recognition Question

The single most important pre-launch credibility step is documented acknowledgment from at least one major resort brand that they are aware of **RAV** and have not objected. This is *not* a partnership agreement, nor is it a marketing deal.

This acknowledgment can be a written statement — even a short email from a brand-protection team contact — that says some version of:

> "We are aware that your platform lists properties associated with our brand; we have not objected to the listings; we expect you to comply with our brand usage guidelines."

This email, kept on file, serves as protection for **RAV** against the eventual cease-and-desist letter that some brand might send when their internal process catches the platform's existence.

nodes.title: APPROACHING TIMESHARE COMPANIES
nodes.body: This is a discussion and ideas on how to approach Timeshare companies and how to make sure we have our ducks in a row to present to them

**Marriott Vacations Worldwide's corporate development team has never refused a meeting because a startup used the wrong incorporation service. **
They have refused meetings because the startup couldn't articulate:

- What its compliance posture was
- What its consumer-protection framework was
- Whether the resort brands were aware of its existence

nodes.title: TCPA Compliance for SMS
nodes.body:
# TCPA Compliance for SMS

Your platform has SMS-based notifications. The context file indicates that SMS is currently in `SMS_TEST_MODE=true` because A2P 10DLC registration is pending, which is the correct posture.

When you turn off test mode and send real SMS to actual consumers, **every recipient must have provided prior express written consent specifically for SMS**, separate from agreeing to the Terms of Service (ToS). This consent should be obtained via a *separate checkbox during signup*.

Additionally, you must **honor opt-outs within 10 days**.

This operational requirement is critical; it is not something that a lawyer's review of policy documents will catch. It represents the highest-dollar risk on the platform.

> One TCPA class action with 10,000 affected users can result in **$5 million to $50 million** in statutory damages.

nodes.title: Timeshare Attorneys to follow up with and key Operations Questions
nodes.body:
Greenberg Traurig has a hospitality        
  practice that handles this, and 
  Holland & Knight has a vacation ownership group
  
  - What is the platform's exposure if a guest checks in and the resort refuses to honor the reservation because the unit's CC&Rs prohibit        
  third-party rental? RAV's ToS needs to address this and PaySafe escrow needs to release funds back to the traveler in this scenario.

- For the 117 resorts in your ResortIQ database: have you obtained or even attempted to obtain documented positions from at least Hilton Grand  Vacations, Marriott Vacations Worldwide, and Disney Vacation Club acknowledging RAV's platform? If the answer is no, that's a pre-launch  must-have.
REF: /boardroom Brian T. Lower — Round 1

One specific operational thing about the Atlas + Mercury bank account combination: 
Mercury will ask for documentation of the business, including a business description. 
Be careful with the language. "We operate a marketplace for timeshare rentals" can flag Mercury's compliance team because timeshare has historically been a high-fraud category. 

Better framing: "Software platform connecting vacation property owners with  travelers; commission-based marketplace; payments processed through Stripe Connect; escrow via PaySafe." 

Get the language right the first time  or you'll have your Mercury account frozen for 2-4 weeks during compliance review.
Brian T. Lower — Round 1

Treating this as a Silicon Valley YC-startup formation when it is actually a  regulated-industry marketplace formation. The legal exposure that will kill RAV is not on the Atlas vs. Gust axis. It's on the "what do the resort brands and Florida regulators think of your platform" axis. Don't let the corporate-formation decision absorb mental energy that should be going to the resort-relationship + regulatory-opinion workstream.

nodes.title: Known competitors to research
nodes.body:
### Adding Posting our first group discussion topic.

##Here is some high level information on things we have to do fro market research. We will have corresponding user stories and maybe a way to organize the information in a useful way soon

#Market Research — Competitors & Positioning


  **Known Competitors to Research**

**1.   - Koala.com — timeshare rental marketplace (you've mentioned them)
2.   - RedWeek.com — one of the oldest timeshare rental/resale platforms
3.   - Timeshares Only (timesharesonly.com) — rental + resale
4.   - SellMyTimeshare.com — has a rental side
5.   - Vacatia — timeshare management/rental hybrid
6.   - VRBO / Airbnb — not timeshare-specific but the giant competitors in vacation rental
7.   - Marriott Vacation Club rentals — direct brand rental programs
8.   - Hilton Grand Vacations rentals — same**

  **What** I'd Suggest for Solid Market Research

  1. Feature comparison matrix —  build a detailed spreadsheet comparing RAV vs. each competitor across: commission rates, booking  
  model (fixed vs bidding), AI features, mobile app, owner tools, traveler tools, resort coverage, pricing transparency
  2. Mystery shopping — Create accounts on Koala, RedWeek, and 2-3 others. Go through their listing and booking flows. Note what's clunky, what's 
  good, what's missing. This is the most valuable research you can do.
  3. Review mining — Check their App Store reviews, Trustpilot, BBB complaints. The negative reviews tell you exactly what problems are unsolved —
   opportunities for RAV.
  4. Google Trends / keyword research — See search volume for "timeshare rental," "rent timeshare week," "vacation ownership rental" to understand
   demand and seasonal patterns.
  5. Timeshare industry reports — ARDA (American Resort Development Association) publishes annual data on the timeshare market (~$10B industry,   
  9.6M owners in the US).
